structured_choices.data_source: Production user interaction logs from the streaming platform, spanning 12 months. Data includes: watch history (title, timestamp, duration), user ratings, session metadata (device, time of day, day of week), and aggregated engagement signals (clicks, completion status, repeat visits). Source: platform data warehouse. Time period: months 1–12 of the calendar year to capture full seasonal cycles.
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary outcome: average watch time per user session (in minutes), aggregated weekly per user. Secondary outcome (for stratification checks): weekly session count and content completion rate (watched >80% of title). Labels are derived directly from platform logs (objective, not self-reported) and measured during the evaluation window only, preventing label leakage.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal + stratified user-level split. Months 1–6 used for training (RL policy initialization and behavior cloning baseline); months 7–9 used for offline validation (policy evaluation without live deployment); months 10–12 held as test set (live A/B test deployment). Stratification: users are grouped by baseline engagement quartile (watch time in month 1) and content genre preference (using LDA on watch history). Within each stratum, users are randomly assigned to control (rule-based, n=50%) or treatment (RL, n=50%) in months 10–12. Rationale: temporal split avoids leakage from future content trends and seasonal effects; stratified assignment ensures balanced confound distribution and detects if algorithm performance varies across user segments; live test validates offline metrics in deployment.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Feature transformers (scaler, encoder for categorical features like genre, device type) are fit on month 1–6 training data only. The same fitted transformers are applied without refitting to months 7–9 validation and months 10–12 test data. This prevents data leakage from validation/test statistics influencing the model. Scaling is applied to continuous features (e.g., normalized session duration, user age, content popularity rank). Categorical features (genre, device) are one-hot encoded. Missing values in watch history are imputed as 'unknown' category (not dropped) to preserve user count.
structured_choices.features: State features (input to RL policy): (1) user history embeddings (last 5 watched titles, encoded via pretrained content embedding model fit on training data only), (2) user profile (genre preference distribution from months 1–6), (3) session context (time of day, day of week, device type, session count in current week), (4) content features (genre, popularity rank, recency, average user rating from training data). Reward signal: watch time delta (minutes watched in recommended session vs. user's historical session median). No feature engineering that depends on test-period labels or future engagement.
structured_choices.model: Contextual bandit RL policy using Thompson Sampling or Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithm, trained on months 1–6 interaction logs via offline RL (batch learning from logged policy gradients). Policy maps context (state features above) to action (content recommendation). Action space: top 500 most-watched titles in training period. Alternatively: a deep Q-network (DQN) or policy gradient method if exploration patterns warrant. Rationale: contextual bandit avoids long-horizon credit assignment problems in streaming (most user sessions are independent); Thompson Sampling naturally balances exploration/exploitation without manual epsilon scheduling.
structured_choices.baseline: Static rule-based system: content ranking by (1) content popularity (watch count in month 1–6), (2) user's historical genre preference match, (3) random tie-breaking. This baseline receives equivalent tuning: rule weights (popularity weight, genre weight) are optimized on months 7–9 validation data by grid search, maximizing average watch time. Baseline is evaluated on the same feature set (context features, content features) and same test set (months 10–12). Rationale: rule-based system is the stated incumbent; tuning it fairly ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of RL/personalization, not of arbitrary baseline neglect.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Average watch time per user session (in minutes) during months 10–12 test period, computed as: (total watch time across all test sessions for user group) / (total number of test sessions for user group). Measured separately for treatment (RL) and control (rule-based) groups. Success criterion: treatment mean watch time is statistically significantly higher than control (two-sided t-test or Mann-Whitney U test, α=0.05, with Bonferroni correction if multiple comparisons). Justification: watch time directly aligns with stakeholder objective (engagement); it is an objective, observable metric; aggregation to session-level averages avoids bias toward power users.
secondary_metrics: Session count per user (proxy for return rate; should not decrease with RL)
Content completion rate (% sessions with >80% watched; indicates relevance)
Diversity of recommendations (Gini coefficient of content distribution; ensures RL does not over-recommend niche titles)
Engagement lift stratified by user segment (baseline engagement quartile, genre preference); detects if RL benefits differ across demographics
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Months 7–9 validation set. RL policy hyperparameters (e.g., Thompson Sampling concentration parameter, exploration bonus) and rule-based baseline weights are tuned by grid search or Bayesian optimization to maximize average watch time on validation data. Validation metrics are computed weekly, and final hyperparameters are frozen before test deployment. Rationale: validation data is temporally distinct from training (months 1–6) and test (months 10–12), avoiding overfitting to test performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Months 10–12 test set is used exactly once, after hyperparameter freeze. During months 10–12, a live A/B test is conducted: users are randomly assigned to treatment or control (stratified by baseline engagement and genre) and served recommendations from either RL or rule-based system. Engagement metrics (watch time, session count, completion rate) are logged in real-time but not used for any model updates or tuning. Analysis occurs only after month 12 is complete.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: variability in user interests and seasonal effects. Controls: (1) stratification by user baseline engagement quartile and genre preference ensures treatment and control groups are balanced on latent user type; (2) temporal split (training on months 1–6, test on months 10–12) is identical for both groups, so both see the same seasonal window; (3) weekly aggregation of watch time reduces noise from individual session variance; (4) analysis includes subgroup performance (primary metric separately for each quartile and genre cluster) to detect confound-driven differences; (5) randomization of user assignment in test phase (stratified) removes confounding in treatment assignment. Residual risk: unobserved user characteristics (e.g., churn propensity, content discovery skill) could correlate with both treatment uptake and engagement, but randomized assignment mitigates this. Limitations: this experiment establishes correlation between RL deployment and engagement in this platform's user population during the test period; it does not establish causation across all platforms or future content catalogs without holdout generalization testing.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RL-Driven Personalization vs. Rule-Based Recommendations

### 1. Data and Temporal Scope

The experiment uses 12 months of production user interaction logs from the streaming platform. The data includes watch history (title, timestamp, duration), user ratings, session context (device, time of day, day of week), and engagement signals (clicks, completion status, repeat visits). This 12-month window is intentionally chosen to capture full seasonal cycles of user behavior and content availability, ensuring that performance differences are not artifacts of seasonal variation.

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal + Stratified

Data is divided into three temporal windows:
- **Training (Months 1–6):** Used to initialize and train the RL policy via offline RL (batch learning from logged interactions). Rule-based baseline parameters are locked using this period.
- **Validation (Months 7–9):** Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. RL policy hyperparameters and rule-based system weights are optimized by grid search on this validation set, maximizing average watch time. This ensures neither model is overfitted to the test set.
- **Test (Months 10–12):** Held as a live A/B test. Users are randomly assigned (stratified by baseline engagement quartile and genre preference) to either treatment (RL recommendations) or control (rule-based recommendations) groups. This separation prevents information leakage and ensures fair evaluation under deployment conditions.

Stratification on user baseline engagement (computed from month 1) and genre preference ensures that treatment and control groups are balanced on key user characteristics, reducing variance and enabling subgroup analysis. Randomization of treatment assignment in the test phase removes confounding from user self-selection.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

State features for the RL policy include: (1) user history embeddings (last 5 watched titles), (2) user genre preference distribution, (3) session context (time of day, device, day of week), and (4) content metadata (genre, popularity rank, average rating). All feature transformers (scaling, encoding) are fit on months 1–6 training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage from future statistics. Categorical features are one-hot encoded; missing values are imputed as 'unknown' to preserve user records.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a **contextual bandit RL policy** using Thompson Sampling or UCB, trained on months 1–6 via offline RL. The policy learns to map user context to content recommendations from a discrete action space (top 500 most-watched titles). Thompson Sampling naturally balances exploration (recommending novel content) and exploitation (recommending known-good content) without manual tuning.

The baseline is a **static rule-based recommender** that ranks content by (1) popularity, (2) genre preference match, and (3) random tie-breaking. Crucially, the baseline is **tuned with equivalent effort**: rule weights are optimized on the validation set (months 7–9) to maximize average watch time, using the same feature set as the RL model. This ensures fair comparison: any performance gap reflects RL's adaptive capability, not from baseline neglect.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is average watch time per session (minutes) during the test period, computed separately for treatment and control groups. Statistical significance is tested using a two-sided t-test or Mann-Whitney U test (α=0.05). Watch time directly measures user engagement as defined by stakeholders and is objective and observable.

**Secondary metrics** include: session count per user (return rate), content completion rate (% sessions with >80% watched), recommendation diversity (Gini coefficient), and **stratified performance** (engagement lift by user baseline quartile and genre). These metrics detect whether RL maintains or improves retention, provides relevant recommendations, avoids homogeneity, and whether benefits are consistent across user segments.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on months 7–9 validation data only. After hyperparameters are frozen, the test set (months 10–12) is touched exactly once for final evaluation. During the test period, a live A/B test is conducted with randomized user assignment (stratified). Metrics are logged in real-time but not used for any model updates; analysis occurs only after the test period ends.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

The known confound—variability in user interests and seasonal effects—is addressed through: (1) stratified randomization, which balances user types across treatment and control; (2) identical temporal windows for both groups (both see months 10–12), so seasonal effects apply equally; (3) subgroup analysis to detect if RL benefits differ across user segments; and (4) randomization of treatment assignment, which removes selection bias. The experiment establishes whether RL-driven personalization causally increases watch time in this user population during the test period; generalization to other platforms or future content requires additional testing.
